structure_blocks.heading: Refund of excess profits
structure_blocks.text: Refund of excess profits

structure_blocks.heading: Refund of excess profits
structure_blocks.text: When audits or evaluations indicate a contractor has earned profits from a contract beyond reasonable levels, it may be necessary for the contractor to return the excess profits. Guidelines for managing such situations, including steps for negotiated and voluntary refunds, addressing refunds from subcontractors, and related tax implications are included below.

structure_blocks.heading: Initial review
structure_blocks.text: Initial review

structure_blocks.heading: Initial review
structure_blocks.text: All instances of excessively high profits from contracts under the Defence Production Act or from non-competitive contracts under the Department of Public Works and Government Services Act should undergo a review. At Public Works and Procurement Canada (PSPC), this review should be carried out in coordination with the director, Acquisition Program Integrity Secretariat (APIS).

structure_blocks.heading: Negotiated refunds
structure_blocks.text: Negotiated refunds

structure_blocks.heading: Negotiated refunds
structure_blocks.text: The initial step for PSPC buyers is to work with APIS to examine available evidence and determine whether the contractor’s profits exceed what is considered fair and reasonable. If evidence is lacking in detail or inconclusive, engaging the contractor for a statement of position or requesting additional verification from the Assurance Services Group (ASG) may be necessary. Once all required evidence is gathered, a final review will be conducted to determine if a refund is warranted and the appropriate method of payment.

structure_blocks.heading: Negotiated refunds
structure_blocks.text: To ensure fairness, PSPC and the Cost and profit Assurance Group (CPAG) will share the buyer’s proposed action plan regarding excess profit identified through an audit with the members of the contract audit review committee. Any concerns with the action plan should be communicated to CPAG within 10 working days. CPAG will then consolidate the feedback and forward it to the lead contracting authority for consideration.

structure_blocks.heading: Negotiated refunds
structure_blocks.text: Recovering excess profits may involve deductions from current claims or partial recovery through an assignment of income tax refund. Typically, contractors are expected to remit the entire amount by cheque. If this causes undue hardship, consideration for extended terms of payment may be considered.

structure_blocks.heading: Negotiated refunds
structure_blocks.text: The final refund amount and settlement terms are outlined in a letter to the contractor, approved by Legal Services, and signed by you, the buyer. A copy of this letter is to be sent to APIS .

structure_blocks.heading: Negotiated refunds
structure_blocks.text: After settlement is completed, it may be desirable to release the contractor from further obligations by detailing, in a formal agreement, the specific contract to which the settlement relates. Legal Services should draft this agreement.

structure_blocks.heading: Negotiated refunds
structure_blocks.text: Contractors should address their cheques to the Receiver General and send them to you, the buyer. You will forward them to CPAG who will forward them to the client's Chief Financial Officer.

structure_blocks.heading: Voluntary refunds
structure_blocks.text: Voluntary refunds

structure_blocks.heading: Voluntary refunds
structure_blocks.text: When you receive notification from a contractor wishing to return excess profits, or if a contractor sends a refund cheque voluntarily, you should request a detailed statement from the contractor. This statement should include:

structure_blocks.heading: Voluntary refunds
structure_blocks.text: a summary of the excess profits by contract

structure_blocks.heading: Voluntary refunds
structure_blocks.text: an explanation of the reasons behind the excess

structure_blocks.heading: Voluntary refunds
structure_blocks.text: the method used to calculate the refund amount

structure_blocks.heading: Voluntary refunds
structure_blocks.text: Pending an appraisal of the information given by the contractor and of the particular circumstances of the case, any cheques received should be sent immediately to the director, APIS , accompanied where possible by a statement, showing the distribution of the refund over the contracts affected. The director, APIS , will then forward the cheques to the Chief Financial Officer for the client.

structure_blocks.heading: Voluntary refunds
structure_blocks.text: The extent of the review for each voluntary refund will be determined by several factors:

structure_blocks.heading: Voluntary refunds
structure_blocks.text: the value of the contracts impacted, and the total amount of the contracts awarded to the contractor

structure_blocks.heading: Voluntary refunds
structure_blocks.text: the contractor's rationale for the excess profits and the methodology used to determine the refund amount

structure_blocks.heading: Voluntary refunds
structure_blocks.text: the contractor’s ability to compile and interpret cost data in line with contract cost principles

structure_blocks.heading: Voluntary refunds
structure_blocks.text: A thorough investigation will be initiated if there is doubt as to the accuracy of the contractor's computations or if it appears that there may be other excess profits which have not been declared.

structure_blocks.heading: Voluntary refunds
structure_blocks.text: The final decision on the refund amount will result from a collaborative discussion between the concerned sector or region and the director, APIS . This decision will then be communicated to the client department's Chief Financial Officer.

structure_blocks.heading: Subcontractor refunds
structure_blocks.text: Subcontractor refunds

structure_blocks.heading: Subcontractor refunds
structure_blocks.text: Handling refunds from subcontractors follows the same guidelines as for prime contractors. It's important for you to maintain communication with the prime contractor about ongoing discussions with the subcontractor. In certain scenarios, it may be more effective to coordinate the refund process through the prime contractor.

structure_blocks.heading: Subcontractor refunds
structure_blocks.text: If the need for a refund comes directly from a contractual agreement between the prime contractor and the subcontractor, then it's the responsibility of the prime contractor to manage the refund process.

structure_blocks.heading: Subcontractor refunds
structure_blocks.text: If the refund arises from circumstances not envisaged in the subcontractor's contractual arrangements with the prime contractor, then the refund should be effected by PSPC , and should not result in a windfall being realized by the prime contractor.

structure_blocks.heading: Income tax refund assignment
structure_blocks.text: Income tax refund assignment

structure_blocks.heading: Income tax refund assignment
structure_blocks.text: In instances where a settlement with a contractor requires partial funding from the contractor's anticipated income tax refund, the procedure is to secure a voluntary agreement from the contractor to assign the income tax refund in the following terms:

structure_blocks.heading: Income tax refund assignment
structure_blocks.text: "Receiver General of Canada Ottawa, Ontario

structure_blocks.heading: Income tax refund assignment
structure_blocks.text: (Input company or contractor name) of the City of (Input city name) in the Province of (Input province name) does hereby authorize and direct that any amounts presently due or accruing due to it in the future from the Canada Revenue Agency, be applied in reduction of its debt to His Majesty the King in right of Canada in the amount of $ (Input dollar amount) on account of (Input account)."

structure_blocks.heading: Corporations
structure_blocks.text: Corporations

structure_blocks.heading: Corporations
structure_blocks.text: In the case of a corporation, the direction should be under the seal of the Corporation and the signature of duly authorized officers. The form, which should be a separate document and not embodied in a letter, should then be passed by the sector/region to the director general, Finance, Corporate Services, for processing in accordance with normal government practice. Treasury Board authorization is not required.

structure_blocks.heading: Notification to Canada Revenue Agency
structure_blocks.text: Notification to Canada Revenue Agency

structure_blocks.heading: Notification to Canada Revenue Agency
structure_blocks.text: Whether the assignment is voluntary or pursuant to section 155 of the Financial Administration Act , the finance sector assumes responsibility for notifying CRA of the assignment.

structure_blocks.heading: Funds transfer
structure_blocks.text: Funds transfer

structure_blocks.heading: Funds transfer
structure_blocks.text: The method of transferring the designated funds from CRA to PSPC or to the Department of National Defence is determined by the finance sector. However, the transfer will be made either by means of a Receiver General cheque or an interdepartmental journal voucher. Under either method, the transfer advice will be passed to the sector/region concerned who will forward it to the director general, finance sector.

raw_text:
Refund of excess profits

When audits or evaluations indicate a contractor has earned profits from a contract beyond reasonable levels, it may be necessary for the contractor to return the excess profits. Guidelines for managing such situations, including steps for negotiated and voluntary refunds, addressing refunds from subcontractors, and related tax implications are included below.

Initial review

All instances of excessively high profits from contracts under the Defence Production Act or from non-competitive contracts under the Department of Public Works and Government Services Act should undergo a review. At Public Works and Procurement Canada (PSPC), this review should be carried out in coordination with the director, Acquisition Program Integrity Secretariat (APIS).

Negotiated refunds

The initial step for PSPC buyers is to work with APIS to examine available evidence and determine whether the contractor’s profits exceed what is considered fair and reasonable. If evidence is lacking in detail or inconclusive, engaging the contractor for a statement of position or requesting additional verification from the Assurance Services Group (ASG) may be necessary. Once all required evidence is gathered, a final review will be conducted to determine if a refund is warranted and the appropriate method of payment.

To ensure fairness, PSPC and the Cost and profit Assurance Group (CPAG) will share the buyer’s proposed action plan regarding excess profit identified through an audit with the members of the contract audit review committee. Any concerns with the action plan should be communicated to CPAG within 10 working days. CPAG will then consolidate the feedback and forward it to the lead contracting authority for consideration.

Recovering excess profits may involve deductions from current claims or partial recovery through an assignment of income tax refund. Typically, contractors are expected to remit the entire amount by cheque. If this causes undue hardship, consideration for extended terms of payment may be considered.

The final refund amount and settlement terms are outlined in a letter to the contractor, approved by Legal Services, and signed by you, the buyer. A copy of this letter is to be sent to APIS .

After settlement is completed, it may be desirable to release the contractor from further obligations by detailing, in a formal agreement, the specific contract to which the settlement relates. Legal Services should draft this agreement.

Contractors should address their cheques to the Receiver General and send them to you, the buyer. You will forward them to CPAG who will forward them to the client's Chief Financial Officer.

Voluntary refunds

When you receive notification from a contractor wishing to return excess profits, or if a contractor sends a refund cheque voluntarily, you should request a detailed statement from the contractor. This statement should include:

a summary of the excess profits by contract

an explanation of the reasons behind the excess

the method used to calculate the refund amount

Pending an appraisal of the information given by the contractor and of the particular circumstances of the case, any cheques received should be sent immediately to the director, APIS , accompanied where possible by a statement, showing the distribution of the refund over the contracts affected. The director, APIS , will then forward the cheques to the Chief Financial Officer for the client.

The extent of the review for each voluntary refund will be determined by several factors:

the value of the contracts impacted, and the total amount of the contracts awarded to the contractor

the contractor's rationale for the excess profits and the methodology used to determine the refund amount

the contractor’s ability to compile and interpret cost data in line with contract cost principles

A thorough investigation will be initiated if there is doubt as to the accuracy of the contractor's computations or if it appears that there may be other excess profits which have not been declared.

The final decision on the refund amount will result from a collaborative discussion between the concerned sector or region and the director, APIS . This decision will then be communicated to the client department's Chief Financial Officer.

Subcontractor refunds

Handling refunds from subcontractors follows the same guidelines as for prime contractors. It's important for you to maintain communication with the prime contractor about ongoing discussions with the subcontractor. In certain scenarios, it may be more effective to coordinate the refund process through the prime contractor.

If the need for a refund comes directly from a contractual agreement between the prime contractor and the subcontractor, then it's the responsibility of the prime contractor to manage the refund process.

If the refund arises from circumstances not envisaged in the subcontractor's contractual arrangements with the prime contractor, then the refund should be effected by PSPC , and should not result in a windfall being realized by the prime contractor.

Income tax refund assignment

In instances where a settlement with a contractor requires partial funding from the contractor's anticipated income tax refund, the procedure is to secure a voluntary agreement from the contractor to assign the income tax refund in the following terms:

"Receiver General of Canada Ottawa, Ontario

(Input company or contractor name) of the City of (Input city name) in the Province of (Input province name) does hereby authorize and direct that any amounts presently due or accruing due to it in the future from the Canada Revenue Agency, be applied in reduction of its debt to His Majesty the King in right of Canada in the amount of $ (Input dollar amount) on account of (Input account)."

Corporations

In the case of a corporation, the direction should be under the seal of the Corporation and the signature of duly authorized officers. The form, which should be a separate document and not embodied in a letter, should then be passed by the sector/region to the director general, Finance, Corporate Services, for processing in accordance with normal government practice. Treasury Board authorization is not required.

Notification to Canada Revenue Agency

Whether the assignment is voluntary or pursuant to section 155 of the Financial Administration Act , the finance sector assumes responsibility for notifying CRA of the assignment.

Funds transfer

The method of transferring the designated funds from CRA to PSPC or to the Department of National Defence is determined by the finance sector. However, the transfer will be made either by means of a Receiver General cheque or an interdepartmental journal voucher. Under either method, the transfer advice will be passed to the sector/region concerned who will forward it to the director general, finance sector.

Refund of excess profits

When audits or evaluations indicate a contractor has earned profits from a contract beyond reasonable levels, it may be necessary for the contractor to return the excess profits. Guidelines for managing such situations, including steps for negotiated and voluntary refunds, addressing refunds from subcontractors, and related tax implications are included below.

The method of transferring the designated funds from CRA to PSPC or to the Department of National Defence is determined by the finance sector.
